risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity)." Comment: The paper explicitly states that participants were randomly assigned, and the review characteristics classify the study as a quasi-randomised controlled trial. However, the abstracted methods in the provided text do not describe the exact sequence-generation method (e.g., computer random numbers, coin toss, etc.). Given the study/review context supplied here, the allocation is treated as random for this domain, but the specific generation procedure is not reported in the text provided.

Summary: The paper states that participants were "randomly assigned to one of two workshops" after baseline measures, but it gives no explicit information about how the assignment was concealed from the person enrolling participants. No central allocation, sealed opaque envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is described. Comment: Because the report describes random assignment but does not explain any method preventing foreknowledge of the next allocation, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "On arriving for the workshops, participants were instructed to complete informed consent and baseline outcome measures... participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity)... Workshops were facilitated by nonlicensed graduate students..." Comment: The study does not report any participant/personnel blinding, and the two active workshops were clearly different and delivered face-to-face, so participants and facilitators would know assignment. Because the outcomes were knowledge, vignette responses, and reported clinical expertise—measures plausibly influenced by awareness of the assigned workshop—performance bias is likely high.

Summary: The outcomes were assessed by participant self-report on written inventories/vignettes (e.g., “Respondents indicated their likelihood of reporting suspected child maltreatment from highly unlikely to highly likely on a 7-point Likerttype scale” and posttest “responses to each of the three instruments”). Because the assessor was the participant, and the measures were objective knowledge items or vignette-based responses scored from written answers rather than clinician judgement, lack of separate blinded outcome assessors is unlikely to have influenced measurement materially. Comment: Although blinding of outcome assessors was not explicitly stated, the nature of the outcomes makes detection bias unlikely in this study.

Quote: "One participant was excluded from the study because this individual was unable to complete the postworkshop assessment." and Table 2 reports "N = 54" for the outcome analyses after "55" participants were enrolled. Comment: Attrition was minimal (1/55, about 2%) and there is no indication that dropout differed by group or was related to the outcomes; the study appears to have analyzed essentially all randomized participants except one who could not complete posttest, so incomplete outcome data are unlikely to bias the results.
